**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the **Activity diagram** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='activity diagram.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\diagrams\activity diagram.mdzip.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e5cea4f-1d53-4b42-b0d7-b3f259c729a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">836520319</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9e6e9acd-0c5a-40d9-b9bf-f3cdb2685d5d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">836520323</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d157e229-6ef6-4d70-8067-0b85a9c70ddc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">836624898</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="da8cac51-d915-497b-86ff-da98cdbf469d"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b46ae91-c3c7-4c33-a6ec-e27faff8e7a3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">836520322</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Description</h3><p>Activity Parameter Nodes are object nodes at the beginning and end of the flows that are used to accept inputs and provide outputs from activities.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of Activity Parameter Nodes in Activity diagram " ac:alt="Example of Activity Parameter Nodes in Activity diagram "><ri:attachment ri:filename="activity-parameter-nodes-in-activity-diagram.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" />  <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><br /></span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Creating an Activity Parameter Node</h3><p>To create an Activity Parameter Node</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Activity diagram palette, click the arrow next to the<strong> Object Node</strong> button to expand the list, then select <strong>Activity Parameter Node</strong>.</li><li>Click the Activity diagram frame. The Activity Parameter Node is created, and its shortcut menu opens.</li><li>Select <strong>Edit New Parameter</strong> to specify the parameter. The <strong>Parameter</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Specify the parameter, and click <strong>Close.</strong><br /><br /></li></ol><p>The parameter for the Activity Parameter Node is created automatically.<br />The parameter name, type, and multiplicity are synchronized with the pin or Object Node from which/to which the Object Flow was created. The direction of the parameter is set according to the Object Flow direction, otherwise you can set it manually by simply right-clicking the activity parameter and selecting the preferred direction.</p><p>When creating an Activity Parameter Node on a diagram frame, click on the diagram frame to open the menu. In the menu, the following Activity Parameter Nodes are listed: </p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li>parameters that are not displayed on the diagram frame. <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li><li>parameters that are owned by the same activity as the activity diagram.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li><li>parameters that have assigned parameters that are also owned by the same activity.</li></ul></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="165" ac:width="261"><ri:attachment ri:filename="activity-node-menu.png" /></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><br /></p><p>If a parameter with an <em>inout</em> direction is selected for the Activity Parameter Node creation, then two Activity Parameter Nodes are created on the diagram frame.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6a7572f8-4ab3-4bf4-b8e6-2a8c37a88a58"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size">You can also create an Activity Parameter Node by drawing an Object Flow directly to or from the Activity diagram frame.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h3>Specifying Activity Parameter Node type</h3><p>Once the Activity Parameter Node is created, you can specify its type. Use one of the following ways:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="button"><ac:link-body>Using the <strong>Specify Type</strong> button.</ac:link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="window"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Using the Specification window.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c81d126-8932-4b4d-bdd6-6d4f9c332dee"><ac:parameter ac:name="">button</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To specify Activity Parameter Node type by using the <strong>Specify Type</strong> button</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>Activity Parameter Node</strong> shape.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:title="Specify Type" ac:thumbnail="true" ac:alt="Specify Type" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specify_type.png" /></ac:image> on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Smart manipulator toolbar" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[smart manipulator toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /> - Select an existing element from the <strong>Select Type</strong> menu.<br /> - Create a new type:<br />        a. Type a name.<br />        b. Press Enter.<br />        c. In the <strong>Create &lt;type name&gt; As </strong>dialog, select the element kind.<br />The type is specified for an Activity Parameter Node.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5886f79e-d17a-44ad-bc15-715d5c44e474"><ac:parameter ac:name="">window</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To specify Activity Parameter Node type by using the Specification window</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Activity Parameter Node and select the <strong>Specification</strong> command.</li><li>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, select the <strong>Type</strong> property value cell.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /> - Select an existing element from the list.<br /> - Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_button_in_specification_window.png" /></ac:image> and in the <strong>Select Element</strong> dialog do the following:<br />         - Select an existing element from the <strong>Tree </strong>or<strong> List</strong> tab.<br />         - create a new element by switching on the <strong>Creation Mode</strong> and clicking the <strong>Create</strong> button.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.<br />The type is specified for an Activity Parameter Node.</li></ol><h3>Setting the direction of an Activity Parameter Node</h3><p>To set the direction of an Activity Parameter Node</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Activity Parameter Node on the diagram and select <strong>Specification</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Parameter</strong> property group, click to expand the <strong>Direction</strong> options drop-down menu.</li><li>Select the preferred direction and click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><h3>Displaying Activity Parameter Nodes</h3><p>You can display Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link> when you are:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="new"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a new Activity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="already_created"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Modifying a pre-existing Activity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ecaa510e-e61f-4ecd-a290-03c160939962"><ac:parameter ac:name="">new</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To display Activity Parameter Nodes when creating a new Activity diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>When creating a new Activity diagram for an Activity element that contains Activity Parameter Nodes, the <strong>Select Parameters</strong> dialog will open automatically.<br /><ac:image ac:title="Automatically opened the Select Parameters dialog when creating a new Cool Room Activity diagram" ac:alt="Automatically opened the Select Parameters dialog when creating a new Cool Room Activity diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_parameters_dialog_when_creating_new_activity_diagram.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p>In the opened<strong> Select Parameters</strong> dialog, select the parameters that you want to display, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />Selected Activity Parameter Nodes are displayed on the frame of the Activity diagram.<br /><ac:image ac:title="Displayed Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame of the Cool Room Activity diagram" ac:alt="Displayed Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame of the Cool Room Activity diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="parameter_nodes-displayed_on_frame_after_creating_new_activity_diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dbdb3dc4-ec3d-4573-bfc9-0c8c4f04f7e9"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want all Activity Parameter Nodes to be displayed automatically when creating a new Activity diagram, go to <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project </strong>on the main menu, and set the <strong>Display All Activity Parameter Nodes in Diagrams</strong> option to <em>true.</em><em><br /></em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea579e1a-d860-43bd-ad58-b3e82b2ed0c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="">already_created</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To display Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame in a pre-existing Activity diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the Activity diagram pane.</li><li>Open the <strong>Select Parameters</strong> dialog by performing one of the following steps:<br /> - From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Display</strong> &gt; <strong>Display Parameter Nodes</strong>.<br /> - On the diagram toolbar, click <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image ac:title="Display" ac:alt="Display"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display.png" /></ac:image></span></span> and select <strong>Display Parameter Nodes.<br /></strong><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Display Parameter Nodes command in the Collect Computer Activity diagram" ac:alt="The Display Parameter Nodes command in the Collect Computer Activity diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="display_parameter_nodes_command.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p>In the dialog, select the parameters you want to display, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Parameters dialog of the Collect Computer Activity diagram" ac:alt="The Select Parameters dialog of the Collect Computer Activity diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_parameters_dialog.png" /></ac:image><br />The selected Activity Parameter Nodes are displayed on the frame of the Activity diagram.<br /><ac:image ac:title="Displayed Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame of the Collect Computer Activity diagram" ac:alt="Displayed Activity Parameter Nodes on the frame of the Collect Computer Activity diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="parameter_nodes-displayed_on_frame.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol><h3>Displaying or hiding the direction of an Activity Parameter Node</h3><p>To display or hide the direction of an Activity Parameter Node</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Activity Parameter Node on the diagram, and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, click to select or clear the <strong>Show Direction</strong> check box to display or hide the direction.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Example of the parameter direction displayed on Activity Parameter Node " ac:alt="Example of the parameter direction displayed on Activity Parameter Node " ac:height="70" ac:width="145"><ri:attachment ri:filename="activity-parameter-node-direction.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6789eb8-2f33-43c5-9a69-aa41b4f188a9"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You can see the direction of the parameter displayed in the <strong>Containment</strong> tree before choosing the parameter name.</li><li>If an Activity Parameter Node is associated with the parameter of an <em>inout</em> direction, then the direction to the Activity Parameter Node is set according to the connected flows. If an outgoing flow is connected to the newly created Activity Parameter Node, then this Activity Parameter Node is set as an input node, and the <em>in</em> parameter direction is used.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e8c27d1-9fc6-4666-ba85-f7c2e75effbf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">836520317</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="three_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /><ac:link-body><span>Activity</span> diagram</ac:link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Node" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Expansion Region and Expansion Nodes" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Loop, Sequence, and Conditional Nodes" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Structured Activity Node" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the <strong><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Activity diagram</span></strong> sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Download<span class="confluence-link"><em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span> <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="activity diagram.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.<br class="confluence-link" /></span></span></em></span></li><li>Find in modeling tool <em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">samples\diagrams</span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">\<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">activity diagram</span>.mdzip.</span></span></em></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227150184 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00037: Actor

- page_id: `227150184`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227150184/Actor
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:52:45.497+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

907312959

907312961

907312960

Actors represent roles played by human users, external hardware, and other subjects. An Actor does not necessarily represent a specific physical entity but merely a particular “role” of some entities that is relevant to an action performed by the Actor. Typically, such actions are called [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR3']. An Actor is represented as a “stick man” figure with the name below the figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can format the actor symbol properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024xR3'].

An Actor can be specified by changing its property values in the Actor [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR3']. Each property is described in the description area on this window.

Actors can be represented in other diagrams, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR3'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR3'].

##### Actor usage in other diagrams

The actor can be used in other diagrams in various ways. Most common cases are as follows:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR3'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space='MD2024xR3'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR3'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Swimlanes' space='MD2024xR3'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR3'] .

lifelineTo create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space='MD2024xR3']for an Actor in a Sequence diagram

1. Open the Sequence diagram wherein you want to create a lifeline.
2. Select an Actor in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR3'] .
3. Drag and drop the selected Actor on the Sequence diagram pane. The Lifeline is created. In the following figure, see an example wherein two Lifelines are depicted.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

swimlaneTo create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Swimlanes' space='MD2024xR3'] header for an Actor in an Activity diagram

1. Open an Activity diagram.
2. Create a Swimlane.
3. Select an Actor in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR3'] .
4. Drag and drop the Actor on the Swimlane header in the Activity diagram. The Actor is assigned to the header of the Swimlane. See the example in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can see the usage of Actors in the example of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024xR3'] in *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\diagrams\use case diagram.mdzip*.

907312958

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="82fa969c-fea2-4cf3-a9cf-25f4b817ccd5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907312959</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90f95b95-7f48-4a57-ae80-dcd82d14196b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907312961</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1c9997e-0a17-4eae-ab24-2d759593701b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907312960</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Actors represent roles played by human users, external hardware, and other subjects. An Actor does not necessarily represent a specific physical entity but merely a particular “role” of some entities that is relevant to an action performed by the Actor. Typically, such actions are called <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Use Cases]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. An Actor is represented as a “stick man” figure with the name below the figure.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of Actor connected to Use Case" ac:alt="Example of Actor connected to Use Case"><ri:attachment ri:filename="actor.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>You can format the actor symbol properties in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Symbol Properties dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>An Actor can be specified by changing its property values in the Actor <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link>. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</p><p>Actors can be represented in other diagrams, such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Sequence]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Activity diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h3>Actor usage in other diagrams</h3><p>The actor can be used in other diagrams in various ways. Most common cases are as follows:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="lifeline"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Actors can be depicted as ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Lifeline" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Lifelines ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <ac:link ac:anchor="lifeline"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[in a Sequence diagram.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="swimlane"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Actors can be depicted as ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Swimlanes" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Swimlane ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <ac:link ac:anchor="swimlane"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[headers in an Activity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link>.</li></ul><p><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="690b59b3-cd57-4518-88c9-32929d2f549b"><ac:parameter ac:name="">lifeline</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Lifeline" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Lifeline ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>for an Actor in a Sequence diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Sequence diagram wherein you want to create a lifeline.</li><li>Select an Actor in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link>.</li><li><p>Drag and drop the selected Actor on the Sequence diagram pane. The Lifeline is created. In the following figure, see an example wherein two Lifelines are depicted.</p></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:title="Lifelines of particular Actor types in Sequence diagram" ac:alt="Lifelines of particular Actor types in Sequence diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="actor_in_sequence.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d10d97b-3203-48bb-acbc-9d71c1ac607b"><ac:parameter ac:name="">swimlane</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Swimlanes" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link> header for an Actor in an Activity diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Open an Activity diagram.</li><li>Create a Swimlane.</li><li>Select an Actor in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Drag and drop the Actor on the Swimlane header in the Activity diagram. The Actor is assigned to the header of the Swimlane. See the example in the following figure.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of Swimlanes that represents Actors" ac:alt="Example of Swimlanes that represents Actors"><ri:attachment ri:filename="actor_in_activity.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33f19f85-f0bc-4930-8504-f75067793072"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can see the usage of Actors in the example of <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link> in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\diagrams\use case diagram.mdzip</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="811aa515-fa9a-4667-8eff-84cf6f4156cb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907312958</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227152163 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00038: Actor and Usecase

- page_id: `227152163`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227152163/Actor+and+Usecase
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-11-15T09:07:17.635+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > UML completeness constraints rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

ActorUsecase

**Description**

This validation rule checks if an Actor is associated with at least one Use Case.

**Severity**

info

**Constrained Element**

Actor

**Solvers**

- To fix this, make sure an Actor is associated with at least one Use Case.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Actor *User* is not associated with any Use Case. 
The error is resolved by drawing an Association relationship between the Actor and the Use Case *Provide comfortable temperature*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>ActorUsecase</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if an Actor is associated with at least one Use Case.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>info</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Actor</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li>To fix this, make sure an Actor is associated with at least one Use Case.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Actor_and_Usecase.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Actor and Usecase" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Actor <em>User</em> is not associated with any Use Case. <br />The error is resolved by drawing an Association relationship between the Actor and the Use Case <em>Provide comfortable temperature</em>.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227150751 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00039: Adding a basic flow step from other use cases

- page_id: `227150751`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227150751/Adding+a+basic+flow+step+from+other+use+cases
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-05-17T08:02:21.459+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case > Use Case Scenario sketch
- labels: ['included-use-case', 'extending-use-case']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

879372979

879372981

879372980

Make sure the selected Use Case has included or extending use cases.

To add a basic flow step from other use cases

1. Open the Use Case Specification window and click the Use Case Scenario Sketch property group.
2. Select the basic flow step where you want to add steps from another Use Case.
3. Click the Add steps from other use cases button [ATTACHMENT filename='add_steps_from_included_use_cases.png'] . The shortcut menu opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='add_new_steps_from_other_use_case.png']
4. Do one of the following:

- 
  - Click Add New Steps From Included Use Case or press ALT+I to add steps from the included Use Case.
  - Click Add New Steps From Extending Use Case or press ALT+E to add steps from the extending Use Case.

Depending on your choice, the **Select Included Use Case**or **Select Extending Use Case**dialog opens.

5. In the opened dialog, select the use case(s) from which you want to add steps to the selected step of the basic flow. Click **OK**after you have selected a Use Case.

- Steps from included use cases are added to the basic flow as substeps of the newly created basic flow step.
- Steps from extending use cases with no extension points use cases are added to the basic flow as substeps of the selected basic flow step.
- Steps from extending use cases with extension points are added as steps of an alternative flow to the selected basic flow step. The extension point is considered the alternative condition.

See the examples in the following figures.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Steps added from included or extending use cases are read-only. You are not allowed to modify them in the current scenario.

You can modify added steps only in use cases from which these steps are added.

879372978

You are not allowed to modify them in the current scenario.</p><p>You can modify added steps only in use cases from which these steps are added.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc89b5b3-7c3d-45f7-b1de-7e90a5a23abf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">879372978</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Transition" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227154850 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00040: Adding a Macro and editing Macro information

- page_id: `227154850`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227154850/Adding+a+Macro+and+editing+Macro+information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T12:01:22.942+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

851898320

851898333

851898317

**On this page:**

851898322

You can add a new macro and enter all necessary information about it by following the steps described in****Opening Macro Information Dialog and**** Adding a Macro and Its Information below.

##### Opening Macro Information Dialog

You can add or modify macro information such as the macro name and description, in the **Macro Information** dialog. To open the **Macro Information** dialog, you need to open the **Organize Macros** dialog first.

To open the **Organize Macros** dialog

- Click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros on the main menu. The Organize Macros dialog will open, see figures below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To open the **Macro Information** dialog

1. Click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros on the main menu to open the Organize Macros dialog, see above figure.
2. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . The Macro Information dialog will open.

##### Adding a Macro and Its Information

Use the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** or **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** button in the **Organize Macros** dialog to add or edit a macro and its information in the **Macro Information** dialog. You can also press the mnemonic keys to add or edit a macro, see****Macro Information Dialog Mnemonic Keys.

To add a macro and enter macro information in the **Macro Information** dialog

1. Open the Organize Macros dialog (click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros on the main menu).
2. Click **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**. The **Macro Information** dialog will open, see figure below. The **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**, **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**, **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**, and **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** buttons in the **Organize Macros** dialog will be disabled if there is no macro in the macro table or if you do not select any macro from the table. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Type the macro name in the Name box.
4. The default macro language you have previously selected (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting a default macro language' space='MD2024xR3'] ) will appear in the Macro Language box.
5. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='button-1.png'] button, to locate a macro file. The Open file dialog will open, see figure below.
6. Select the file and its type (there are 5 types of file filter: *.bsh , *.groovy , *.rb , *.js , or *.py ), see figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='open-dialog.png']
7. Click Open, see figure above. The selected pathname will appear in the File box in the Macro Information dialog, see figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='fig 8.jpg'] [ATTACHMENT filename='macro-information.png']
8. Select either (i) the **Use path variables** check box or (ii) the **Add macro to model** check box. If you have specified the file or network path in the **Environment Options** dialog by clicking **Options** > **Environment** > **Path** **Variables** and selected the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box in **Macro Information** dialog, the <Path Variable name> will show in front of the file pathname. This field is the [Required] field, for example, *<mypath>/<macro_name>.js.*If you select the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box, the full pathname will not be saved.If you select the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box, your source code will be imported from the file to the model. The location to keep the model is Data::MacroEngine.You can open the **Macro Information** by using Ctrl+Alt+m as a shortcut key.
9. Type the macro description in the **Description** box, see figure above. A macro name must be unique and cannot be duplicated.
10. The function of the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box is to allow you to run the default values automatically. If you select the check box, the system will not open a dialog to prompt you to input the value. If you select the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box, you need to enter the valid default value of each parameter.
11. Click **Add** to specify the arguments of the macro. The arguments specified in the **Arguments** table will be the global variables of a specific macro. A parameter type can be a String, Integer, Long, Double, Date, or ElementPath.If the **Null** check box is selected, you cannot enter the default value of that particular parameter.An empty value in the **Default Value** column does not necessarily mean a null value, for example an empty string value is an empty string.If you input an invalid default value or you do not enter the argument name, the system will display the following error message when you run the script: **The following argument(s) are invalid: <List of the invalid argument>**.
  - Name column contains the name of a parameter
  - Type column contains the type of a parameter
  - Array check box is to identify if an array is the parameter
  - Null check box is to identify if null is the parameter value
  - Default Value column contains an initial value to run the macro
12. Type a keyboard shortcut that will be used to run the macro in the [ATTACHMENT filename='press-new-shortcut-key.png'] box and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . The newly assigned keyboard shortcut will appear in the Current keys box.
13. Click **OK** . The **Organize Macros** dialog will open, showing the newly added macro name, description, and keyboard shortcut. Macro names, filenames, and languages are required.Macro description and keyboard shortcuts are optional.If any of the required fields are not entered or duplicate macro name is entered, the following message will open: **The following field(s) are invalid: <List of the problems>**.If the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box is selected and at least one value is empty or not valid, then the following error message will open: **The following value(s) are invalid. <List of the invalid value>**If the****[IMAGE alt='' src='']**** check box is selected, all variables must have valid values or are set to null.
14. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to close the Organize Macros dialog.

##### Editing Macro Information

You can see macro information such as names and descriptions, as well as the macro keyboard shortcuts in the **Organize Macros** dialog.

To edit macro information

1. Click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros . The Organize Macros dialog will open, see figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Select a macro from the table and either click **Edit** or press **Alt + E**. The **Macro Information** dialog will open.

3. Edit the macro information.

4. Click **OK** to save it. You will see the altered information in the **Organize Macros** dialog.

5. Click **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**.

##### Macro Information Dialog Mnemonic Keys

Macro Engine provides mnemonic keys for you to perform some operations, for example, highlight a textbox and click a specific button in the **Macro Information** dialog. The table below lists the**Macro Information** dialog mnemonic keys and their function. Below is a table listing of Macro Information Dialog Mnemonic Keys.

| Mnemonic keys | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Alt + N | To place the pointer in the box. |
| Alt + M | To highlight the box. |
| Alt + F | To place the pointer in the box. |
| Alt + . | To open the File dialog. |
| Alt + D | To place the pointer in the box. |
| Alt + A | To add an argument in the Arguments box. |
| Alt + l | To delete an argument in the Arguments area. |
| Alt + U | To highlight the Current keys box. |
| Alt + G | To click the button. |
| Alt + R | To click the button. |
| Alt + E | To click the button. |
| Alt + P | To place the pointer in the box. |
| Alt + O | To click the button. |
| Alt + C | To click the button. |
| Alt + H | To click the button. |

The <strong>Open file</strong> dialog will open, see figure below.</li><li>Select the file and its type (there are 5 types of file filter: <em>*.bsh</em>, <em>*.groovy</em>, <em>*.rb</em>, <em>*.js</em>, or <em>*.py</em>), see figure below.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Use Path Variables Checkbox" ac:alt="Use Path Variables Checkbox"><ri:attachment ri:filename="open-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>Open, </strong>see figure above. The selected pathname will appear in the <strong>File</strong> box in the <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog, see figure below.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Types of File Filter" ac:alt="Types of File Filter"><ri:attachment ri:filename="fig 8.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br /><span> <br /></span> <br /><ac:image ac:title="Macro Information Dialog" ac:alt="Macro Information Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="macro-information.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p><span>Select either (i) the </span> <strong>Use path variables</strong> <span> check box or (ii) the </span> <strong>Add macro to model</strong> <span> check box.</span> <span> <br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d5416dd1-742d-4ab3-b82c-babe0c89e2ad"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>If you have specified the file or network path in the <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog by clicking <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Environment</strong> &gt; <strong>Path</strong> <strong>Variables</strong> and selected the <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="use-path-variables.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> check box in <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog, the &lt;Path Variable name&gt; will show in front of the file pathname. This field is the [Required] field, for example, <em>&lt;mypath&gt;/&lt;macro_name&gt;.js.</em></li><li>If you select the <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="use-path-variables.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> check box, the full pathname will not be saved.</li><li>If you select the <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add-macro-to-model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> check box, your source code will be imported from the file to the model. The location to keep the model is Data::MacroEngine.</li><li>You can open the <strong>Macro Information</strong> by using Ctrl+Alt+m as a shortcut key.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><span>Type the macro description in the </span> <strong>Description</strong> <span> box, see figure above.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="86396671-f89d-4b01-b34b-49dc07693d2d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>A macro name must be unique and cannot be duplicated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><span>The function of the </span> <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatically-run-with-defaults.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> <span> check box is to allow you to run the default values automatically. If you select the check box, the system will not open a dialog to prompt you to input the value.</span> <br /><span> <br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dddf5cb2-6c91-46b2-a606-b7bf9a4c85f4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you select the <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatically-run-with-defaults.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> check box, you need to enter the valid default value of each parameter.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><span>Click </span> <strong>Add</strong> <span> to specify the arguments of the macro. The arguments specified in the </span> <strong>Arguments</strong> <span> table will be the global variables of a specific macro.</span></p><ul><li><strong>Name</strong> column contains the name of a parameter</li><li><strong>Type</strong> column contains the type of a parameter</li><li><strong>Array</strong> check box is to identify if an array is the parameter</li><li><strong>Null</strong> check box is to identify if null is the parameter value</li><li><strong>Default Value</strong> column contains an initial value to run the macro</li></ul><p><span> <br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9cf0edb8-a556-4ad8-bb80-2c0f70a2dc3a"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>A parameter type can be a String, Integer, Long, Double, Date, or ElementPath.</li><li>If the <strong>Null</strong> check box is selected, you cannot enter the default value of that particular parameter.</li><li>An empty value in the <strong>Default Value</strong> column does not necessarily mean a null value, for example an empty string value is an empty string.</li><li>If you input an invalid default value or you do not enter the argument name, the system will display the following error message when you run the script: <strong>The following argument(s) are invalid: &lt;List of the invalid argument&gt;</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span>Type a keyboard shortcut that will be used to run the macro in the</span> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="press-new-shortcut-key.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <span> box and click </span> <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="assign-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> <span>. The newly assigned keyboard shortcut will appear in the Current keys box.</span></li><li><p><span>Click </span> <strong>OK</strong> <span>. The </span> <strong>Organize Macros</strong> <span> dialog will open, showing the newly added macro name, description, and keyboard shortcut.</span> <span> <br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38da4285-f9d0-4826-8812-5566b56beb29"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Macro names, filenames, and languages are required.</li><li>Macro description and keyboard shortcuts are optional.</li><li>If any of the required fields are not entered or duplicate macro name is entered, the following message will open: <strong>The following field(s) are invalid: &lt;List of the problems&gt;</strong>.</li><li>If the <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatically-run-with-defaults.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> check box is selected and at least one value is empty or not valid, then the following error message will open: <strong>The following value(s) are invalid. &lt;List of the invalid value&gt;</strong></li><li>If the<strong> <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatically-run-with-defaults.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> </strong> check box is selected, all variables must have valid values or are set to null.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span>Click </span> <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="close-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> <span> to close the </span> <strong>Organize Macros</strong> <span> dialog.</span></li></ol><h3>Editing Macro Information</h3><p>You can see macro information such as names and descriptions, as well as the macro keyboard shortcuts in the <strong>Organize Macros</strong> dialog.</p><p><br /></p><p>To edit macro information</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Organize Macros</strong>. The Organize Macros dialog will open, see figure below.</li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Editing a Macro" ac:alt="Editing a Macro"><ri:attachment ri:filename="organize-macros.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>      2.  Select a macro from the table and either click <strong>Edit</strong> or press <strong>Alt + E</strong>. The <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog will open.</p><p>      3.  Edit the macro information.</p><p>      4.  Click <strong>OK</strong> to save it. You will see the altered information in the <strong>Organize Macros</strong> dialog.</p><p>      5.  Click <strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="close-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong>.</p><h3>Macro Information Dialog Mnemonic Keys</h3><p>Macro Engine provides mnemonic keys for you to perform some operations, for example, highlight a textbox and click a specific button in the <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog. The table below lists the<strong> Macro Information</strong> dialog mnemonic keys and their function. Below is a table listing of Macro Information Dialog Mnemonic Keys.</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup> <col /> <col /> </colgroup><thead style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th><p>Mnemonic keys</p></th><th><p> Function</p></th></tr></thead><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td><strong>Alt + N</strong></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>To place the pointer in the <span>box.</span> <br /><strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="name-box.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong> </p></div></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td><strong>Alt + M</strong></td><td><div the <span>box.</span> <br /><strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="descrip-box.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong></p></div></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td colspan="1"><strong>Alt + A</strong></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>To add an argument in the <strong>Arguments </strong>box.<strong> <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="argument-area.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong></p></div></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td colspan="1"><strong>Alt + l</strong></td><td colspan="1">To delete an argument in the <strong>Arguments</strong> area.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td colspan="1"><strong>Alt + U</strong></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>To highlight ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong></p></div></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td colspan="1"><strong>Alt + G</strong></td><td colspan="1"><div <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227151323 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00041: Adding a new Metric Suite

- page_id: `227151323`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227151323/Adding+a+new+Metric+Suite
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-02-19T08:53:41.502+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Metric Suites
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

189812560

189812562

189812561

There are two ways to add a new Metric Suite to your model:

- Create a Metric Suite using the BaseMetricSuite (recommended).
- Create a Metric Suite from scratch.

The *BaseMetricSuite* is a predefined Metric Suite stored in the *DSL Customization* profile of the *UML Standard Profile*.

You are free to choose either way, but we highly recommend the first one. It aids the creation of a new Metric Suite, which inherits the *BaseMetricSuite*attributes that are necessary for calculating metrics (e.g., the context parameter definition and the metric definition for date tracking). This way you don’t have to specify them on your own.

To create a Metric Suite using the *BaseMetricSuite*

1. Make sure that your project uses the UML Standard Profile . If not, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using other projects in a project' space='MD2024xR3'] by going to Options > Project Usages .
2. Create a Package to store all the Metric Suites of your model.
3. In the Package, create a Profile Diagram. If you can't find it in the list of available diagrams, enable the Expert mode.
4. Press Ctrl+Alt+F and in the **Quick Find** dialog type “BMS”. Make sure you clear the **Apply Filter** check box.
5. Click the *BaseMetricSuite* in the list of search results. The *BaseMetricSuite* is selected in the Containment tree.
6. Drag the *BaseMetricSuite* from the Containment tree to the diagram pane. Its shape appears on the diagram.
7. In the diagram palette, click **MetricSuite**, and then click a free space on the diagram. A new metric suite is created and its shape appears on the diagram.
8. Create a Generalization relationship from the newly created Metric Suite to the *BaseMetricSuite.*

Now the newly created Metric Suite inherits all the parameter and metric definitions of the *BaseMetricSuite.*

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a Metric Suite from scratch

1. Create a Package to store all the Metric Suites of your model.
2. In the Package, create a Profile Diagram. If you can't find it in the list of available diagrams, enable the Expert mode.
3. On the diagram palette, click MetricSuite , and then click a free space on the diagram.

A new Metric Suite is created and its shape appears on the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To specify the description of a Metric Suite

1. Double-click the shape of a Metric Suite to open its Specification window.
2. Click Documentation/Hyperlinks on the left side of the window.
3. In the text box on the right side of the window, type the description.
4. Click Close .

189812558

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metric Suites' space='MD2024xR3']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specify the target for the Metric Suite' space='MD2024xR3']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying parameter definitions' space='MD2024xR3']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Building metric definitions' space='MD2024xR3']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Building validation-based metric definitions' space='MD2024xR3']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specific use cases' space='MD2024xR3']

This way you don’t have to specify them on your own.<br /> </p><p>To create a Metric Suite using the <em>BaseMetricSuite</em></p><hr /><ol><li>Make sure that your project uses the <em>UML Standard Profile</em>. If you can't find it in the list of available diagrams, enable the <strong>Expert</strong> mode.</li><li>On the diagram palette, click <strong>MetricSuite</strong>, and then click a free space on the diagram.</li></ol><p>A new Metric Suite is created and its shape appears on the diagram.<br /> </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="new_metric_suite.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a new Metric Suite" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>To specify the description of a Metric Suite</p><hr /><ol><li>Double-click the shape of a Metric Suite to open its Specification window.</li><li>Click <strong>Documentation/Hyperlinks</strong> on the left side of the window.</li><li>In the text box on the right side of the window, type the description.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be351d3f-f0c0-4156-a9a2-4100f77fdee7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">189812558</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Metric Suites" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specify the target for the Metric Suite" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying parameter definitions" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Building metric definitions" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Building validation-based metric definitions" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specific use cases" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227154932 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00042: Adding a scripting language to the modeling tool

- page_id: `227154932`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227154932/Adding+a+scripting+language+to+the+modeling+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:47:56.030+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can add a new scripting language that supports JSR-223 to the modeling tool by editing the Macro Engine's *plugin.xml* file.

To add a new scripting language

1. Open the plugin.xml file in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/plugin.xml .
2. Add the following jar file tags to the runtime section and save the file.

```text
"/>]]>
```

The path to the jar file is relative to *<modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton* or the absolute pathname can be used, for example, if you want to add Sleep programming language, add the following tag to the runtime section:

```text
]]>
```

A slash (/) is used as a directory separator for all platforms, including Windows. The new language will be displayed in most of the Macro Engine dialogs, for example, in the Macros section in the **Environment Options** dialog, the**Create Macro** dialog, and in the**Macro Information** dialog. The language name is automatically configured from the information inside the jar file. This version of Macro Engine does not allow you to modify the language name.

##### Script Filename Extension Filter

When you open an **Open** file dialog to browse for a script file in Macro Engine, you can find the filename extension for the script file in the dialog file filter. If you have added a new scripting language to the modeling tool, for example, Sleep programming language, Macro Engine will add the filename extension (**.sl*)” in the**Open** file dialog.

The filename extensions are derived from jar files. There can be more than one filename extension for each engine. However, the current Macro Engine version only shows the first filename extension that is queried from the engine jar file.

You can remove a scripting language from Macro Engine by removing the associated library tag in the *plugin.xml* file. Once the tag has been removed, the language will be removed from all of the dialogs in Macro Engine. However, the script configured to be used with the language will not be removed from the modeling tool. The language of the script will be reset to the default language, which has been previously configured in the modeling tool**Environment Options**dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The language name is automatically configured from the information inside the jar file. This version of Macro Engine does not allow you to modify the language name.</p><h3>Script Filename Extension Filter</h3><p>When you open an <strong>Open</strong> file dialog to browse for a script file in Macro Engine, you can find the filename extension for the script file in the dialog file filter. If you have added a new scripting language to the modeling tool, for example, Sleep programming language, Macro Engine will add the filename extension (<em>*.sl</em>)” in the<strong> Open</strong> file dialog.</p><p>The filename extensions are derived from jar files. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227149551 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00043: Adding and removing rows

- page_id: `227149551`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227149551/Adding+and+removing+rows
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-07-08T08:30:51.513+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Basic tasks in tables > Working with rows
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Adding a new row

You can create a new element in the model by adding a new row in the table. Each type of table requires a different procedure to add new row(s).

To add a new row in the Generic and Glossary tables

1. On the table toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='add_new.png'] button.

#### INFO: More information

More information

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Glossary table' space='MD2024xR3']]]>

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space='MD2024xR3']]]>

To add a new row in the Instance Table

1. On the table toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='instance_table.png'] > [ATTACHMENT filename='button_create.png']

#### INFO: More information

More information

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance table' space='MD2024xR3']]]>

To add a new row in the Metric Table

1. On the table toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='calculate_metrics.png'] > [ATTACHMENT filename='add_new_metric_with_different_parameters.png']

#### INFO: More information

More information

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metric table' space='MD2024xR3']]]>

To add an existing element as a row in the Generic, Instance, Glossary, Requirement, and Metric tables

1. In the table toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='add_existing.png'] button.
2. Select the element existing in the model and click OK .

##### Removing a row from a table

To remove a row from a table

1. Select the whole row or select one cell of the row you want to remove.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click the Remove From Table button on the table toolbar.
  - Press Ctrl+Delete on the keyboard.

##### Removing an element from a model

You can delete an element from the model when removing the row from a table.

To remove an element from a table

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting an entire row' space='MD2024xR3'] or select one cell of the row you want to remove.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click the Delete button on the table toolbar.
  - Press Ctrl+D on the keyboard.

## PAGE 00044: Adding JRuby and installing Gems

- page_id: `227154930`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227154930/Adding+JRuby+and+installing+Gems
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T15:08:27.839+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

147042186

147042197

147042185

**On this page:**

147042196

By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, you can add it manually. In addition, you can also install Gems for JRuby.

##### Adding JRuby to the modeling tool

To add JRuby to the modeling tool

1. Copy the JRuby engine jar file (e.g. jruby-complete-9.2.14.0.jar ) to <*tool_installation_directory*>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\lib.
2. Add the entry below to the file *<tool_installation_directory>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\enginedescriptor.xml.* rb
 
 JRuby
]]>
3. Append a library entry in file *<*tool_installation_directory*>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\plugin.xml.* ]]>

##### Installing Gems for JRuby

To install a gem for Ruby engine in the modeling tool

1. Open the command line.
2. Go to the folder <*tool_installation_directory*>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\lib.
3. Enter the following command to install a gem.

```text
.jar -S gem install [--user-install]   ...]]>
```

- <version> is the JRuby version, e.g. 9.2.14.0.
- <gem name> is the name of a gem.
- The three dots (...) mean that you can type a list of gem names there.

Once the gem has been installed, you can use it in the Macro Engine. The example below shows how to create a macro that can generate a unique ID by using a gem.

```text

```

To install a gem for the existing Ruby environment on your machine and use it in the Macro Engine

1. Add the following properties to specify the home and library of JRuby in <**tool_installation_directory**>/bin/mduml.properties under JAVA_ARGS section (assuming you have JRuby installed in C:/jruby-9.2.14.0 on your machine).

```text

```

For example:

```text

```

2. Change the JRuby library path to navigate to your *jruby.jar* in *<***tool_installation_directory***>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/plugin.xml*.

```text
]]>
```

1. The command in this section should run in <****tool_installation_directory****>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engine .
2. If you have a whitespace in the property file path, you need to type a double quote to wrap both the property and its value, for each property, for example: “ -Djruby.home=C:/my white space/jruby-*9.2.14.0* " “ -Djruby.lib=C:/my white space/jruby-*9.2.14.0*/lib ”

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227153847 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00045: Adding more report data directories

- page_id: `227153847`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227153847/Adding+more+report+data+directories
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T14:14:32.915+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Configuring Report Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

267209495

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

267209497

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

267209496

The default report data directories for operating systems are as follows:

- For Windows: text\AppData\Local\.magicdraw\\data\reports]]>
- For Linux: text/./< modeling tool version number>]]>

Report Wizard provides a configuration to add more report data directories by creating a **report.properties** file in the Report Wizard plugin directory, *<modeling tool name>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard*.

You can declare the **template_loc** parameter in the **report.properties** file to specify another report data directory as follows:

```text

```

You can also specify the **template_loc** parameter for multiple report data directories by using a semicolon (;) to separate each of the directories.

```text

```

All of templates in the default report data directory and other locations specified in the **report.properties** file are available for generating in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard dialog' space='MD2024xR3']**Report Wizard** dialog and can be called through the **-template** parameter in command line report generation.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The template names defined in the **template.xml** file in each report data directory must be unique.

267209494

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Configuring Report Wizard' space='MD2024xR3'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Configuring Report Wizard' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard Environment options' space='MD2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

ac:name="id">267209495</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c310c76d-11e3-4078-b678-afc96d69fe47"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">267209497</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="17315c06-c981-4548-8a01-c24991f40ddb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">267209496</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The default report data directories for operating systems are as follows:</p><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">For Windows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a28ec22e-0f85-40e6-bebd-e1a4b8876d64"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Local\.magicdraw\<version>\data\reports]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">For Linux:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fce20ea5-e910-4575-9da8-ddd603aff26e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<user.home>/.<modeling tool name>/< modeling tool version number>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><p>Report Wizard provides a configuration to add more report data directories by creating a <strong>report.properties</strong> file in the Report Wizard plugin directory, <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard</em>.</p><p><br />You can declare the <strong>template_loc</strong> parameter in the <strong>report.properties</strong> file to specify another report data directory as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="51e6feb7-8bce-4f41-a6f5-28c87018c62b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[template_loc=C:/Users/MD_user/AppData/Local/.magicdraw/new Location]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>You can also specify the <strong>template_loc</strong> parameter for multiple report data directories by using a semicolon (;) to separate each of the directories.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="29ffda9b-ba60-4eba-a498-1c26974a6010"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[template_loc=//template_server/MagicReport/;C:/Users/MD_user/AppData/Local/.magicdraw/new Location]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>All of templates in the default report data directory and other locations specified in the <strong>report.properties</strong> file are available for generating in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard dialog" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:link-body><strong>Report Wizard</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> and can be called through the <strong>-template</strong> parameter in command line report generation.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="14238e77-8e36-45c5-b275-31b394f131be"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The template names defined in the <strong>template.xml</strong> file in each report data directory must be unique.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e953e83d-035d-4301-89e4-3da381fb43b9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">267209494</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Configuring Report Wizard" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configuring Report]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Configuring Report Wizard" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ Wizard]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard Environment options" ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227149632 space=MD2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00046: Adding or removing element types

- page_id: `227149632`
- space_key: `MD2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR3/pages/227149632/Adding+or+removing+element+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-03-23T12:51:09.750+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Generic table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To add or remove element types

1. In the Criteria area, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='edit_button.png'] button next to the Element Type box. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting element types' space='MD2024xR3']**Select Element Type** dialog with the element and stereotype list opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='criteria_element_type.png']
2. Do one of the following:

- To add the element types, select the appropriate check boxes in the list. Properties corresponding to the selected types will be added to the list of available columns.
- To remove the element types, click to clear the appropriate check boxes in the list. Properties corresponding to the deselected types will be removed from the list of available columns. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Select the Include subtypes check box to display the subtypes of the selected element types.

- Select the Include Custom Types check box to display the custom types extending the selected element types.

To add an element type, you can also drag a selected element to the **Element Type** box directly from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR3'].

- To select adjacent elements, select a single element and hold down the Shift key while you click other elements.
- To select nonadjacent elements, select a single element and hold down the Ctrl key while you click other elements.

The new value of the **Element Type** box replaces the old value.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
